author: Dr. Anna Reyes (PGY-2) / Dr. Jennifer Beckman (Medicine attending)
content:
DISCHARGE SUMMARY — JOHNS HOPKINS HOSPITAL
Date/Time: 4/12/2027, 8:00 AM (Hospital Day 57)
Patient: Nguyen, Lan T (MRN: MRN-90215888)
Admission date: 2/15/2027
Discharge date: 4/12/2027 (Hospital Day 57)
Length of stay: 57 days
Discharge disposition: FutureCare Charles Village SNF (admission anticipated 4/12 11 AM)
Interpreter: Vietnamese phone interpreter used for the discharge teaching with patient and son this morning.

ADMISSION DIAGNOSIS:
Severe acute biliary pancreatitis with biliary sepsis.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSES:
1. Severe acute biliary pancreatitis (resolved) — status post emergent ERCP with sphincterotomy and CBD stone extraction 2/16/2027.
2. Biliary sepsis (E. coli, biliary source) — resolved with antibiotics (pip/tazo x 13 days).
3. Moderate acute respiratory distress syndrome — resolved; status post mechanical ventilation 2/17–2/27.
4. Acute kidney injury on chronic kidney disease — resolved; back to baseline Cr 1.3.
5. Right lower extremity proximal deep vein thrombosis (acute, provoked) — diagnosed 3/1; on therapeutic apixaban day 40.
6. ICU-acquired weakness / severe deconditioning — improving with rehabilitation.
7. Cholelithiasis (chronic, symptomatic) — known prior to admission; outpatient elective cholecystectomy planned.
8. Chronic kidney disease, stage 3a — baseline; preserved during admission.
9. Hypertension — well-controlled.
10. Hyperlipidemia — well-controlled.
11. Latent tuberculosis infection (untreated) — known; active TB ruled out this admission.
12. Anemia, chronic (CKD-related) — at baseline, improving from ICU nadir.
13. Penicillin allergy (mild childhood rash, non-anaphylactic).
14. Iodinated contrast allergy (urticarial — pre-medication required for future contrast).

BRIEF HOSPITAL COURSE:

Ms. Nguyen is a 72-year-old Vietnamese-speaking woman with a history of cholelithiasis (surgery declined), CKD III, HTN, HLD, latent TB infection, right TKA (2018), and former smoking (~14 pack-years, quit 2004) who was admitted 2/15/2027 with severe acute biliary pancreatitis. Her course was complicated as follows:

• HD 1-2 (2/15-2/16): Severe biliary pancreatitis with developing sepsis. Emergent ERCP 2/16 with sphincterotomy and CBD stone extraction; biliary fluid grew E. coli. Started on pip/tazo.
• HD 2-13 (2/16-2/27): MICU admission for septic shock and moderate ARDS. Intubated 2/17, extubated 2/27. Off vasopressors by HD 4. AKI on CKD developed, peaked Cr 2.6, fully recovered. Active TB rule-out with 3 AFB smears + NAAT all negative; airborne isolation discontinued HD 8.
• HD 13-22 (2/27-3/8): Step-down. Weaning O2, rehabilitating from ICU-acquired weakness.
• HD 16 (3/2): New right lower extremity proximal DVT diagnosed. Initiated heparin gtt; transitioned to apixaban 10 mg BID x 7 d then 5 mg BID maintenance (current day 40 of treatment, ~3-month course planned).
• HD 22-26 (3/8-3/12): Transferred to general medicine floor 3/12.
• HD 26-57 (3/12-4/12): Continued rehabilitation. Advanced from rolling walker with assist to independent ambulation with rolling walker on flat surfaces and supervised stair-climbing. Anticipated post-discharge rehab at SNF for further functional recovery.

DISCHARGE EXAM:
Vitals: BP 128/76, HR 76, RR 14, T 98.0 F, SpO2 96% RA. Weight 57.4 kg.
General: Pleasant, deconditioned but markedly improved from ICU course.
HEENT: No icterus. Mucous membranes moist.
CV: RRR, no murmur.
Lungs: CTAB.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended.
Extremities: No edema. R LE: residual mild trace edema, no tenderness.
MSK: Significant proximal muscle wasting; functional improvement ongoing.
Neuro: Alert, oriented x 3 via interpreter. No focal deficits.

DISCHARGE LABS (4/12, AM):
- CBC: WBC 6.4, Hgb 10.5, Plt 256
- BMP: Na 138, K 3.9, Cl 102, CO2 26, BUN 22, Cr 1.3, Glu 96
- LFTs: AST 24, ALT 22, ALP 78, T-bili 0.5, Albumin 3.2
- INR 1.0
- Magnesium 1.9, Phosphorus 3.4, Calcium 9.2

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS:
1. Apixaban 5 mg PO BID (continue for ~3 months total from 3/2/2027; reassess at hematology follow-up 5/15)
2. Lisinopril 5 mg PO daily (continue; PCP may re-titrate; BP currently well-controlled)
3. Atorvastatin 40 mg PO daily
4. Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO q6h PRN pain (max 3 g/day given low albumin)
5. Calcium 600 mg PO daily + Vit D3 1000 IU daily
6. Multivitamin PO daily
7. Potassium chloride 20 mEq PO daily (continue; PCP to reassess at follow-up)
8. Pantoprazole 40 mg PO daily (continue x 1 month total; discontinue at PCP follow-up if no GI symptoms)

DISCONTINUED MEDICATIONS:
- Ibuprofen (do NOT use given CKD + anticoagulation; substitute acetaminophen for pain)

ALLERGIES (re-documented for any post-discharge providers):
- Penicillin (mild childhood rash, non-anaphylactic; tolerated pip/tazo this admission)
- IODINATED CONTRAST (urticarial in 2019; PRE-MEDICATION REQUIRED for any future contrast — prednisone 50 mg PO at 13, 7, and 1 hour pre-procedure plus diphenhydramine 50 mg PO 1 hour pre-procedure)

DIET / ACTIVITY:
- Regular diet, low-sodium (DASH-style), low-fat (gallbladder protection until cholecystectomy)
- Activity as tolerated; will continue active PT/OT at SNF
- Family-cooked Vietnamese foods are fine — counseled on avoiding heavy fried/fatty meals until after cholecystectomy.

FOLLOW-UP APPOINTMENTS (all booked, Vietnamese interpreter arranged for each):
1. Dr. Phong Le, PCP (Charm City Asian Health Center): 5/2/2027 at 9:00 AM. Phone (410) 555-3300.
2. Hematology (Dr. Pishko): 5/15/2027 at 10:30 AM. Phone (410) 955-XXXX.
3. General Surgery (Dr. Markovich): 6/1/2027 at 1:30 PM. Phone (410) 955-XXXX. For discussion of elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
4. Outpatient labs (CBC, CMP, INR/aPTT, lipid panel): 5/1/2027 (day before PCP visit) — Quest patient service center, Charles Village location.
5. SNF physician will see weekly during rehab stay (FutureCare Charles Village medical director).

RETURN-TO-ED PRECAUTIONS (provided in Vietnamese, written + verbal teach-back):
- Severe abdominal pain (especially RUQ/epigastric)
- Fever ≥100.4 F or shaking chills
- Yellowing of eyes or skin (jaundice)
- Worsening leg swelling, redness, or pain
- Shortness of breath, chest pain
- Any bleeding that doesn't stop (nose, gums, cuts), bloody or black stools, blood in urine
- Severe headache or focal neurologic symptoms
- Falls
- Confusion

ADVANCE CARE PLANNING:
Code status: FULL CODE.
Healthcare proxy: Quang Nguyen (son). Form completed 4/8/2027, scanned in chart.

FAMILY EDUCATION COMPLETED:
Vietnamese-language discharge instructions provided to patient and son Quang. Teach-back confirmed understanding of:
- Medication purpose, doses, timing, bleeding precautions on apixaban.
- Diet recommendations.
- Follow-up appointment schedule.
- Return-to-ED warning signs.
- Cholecystectomy planning for ~6/2027.

This 57-day admission was complex but Ms. Nguyen has emerged ambulatory, off oxygen, and with restored baseline renal function. The remaining major recovery task is functional reconditioning, addressed at the SNF. Her gallbladder will need to be removed electively in the coming months.

Thank you to all the consulting services involved in her care: MICU, ID, Hematology, Pulmonary, GI, General Surgery, PT/OT, Nutrition, Case Management, Social Work.

This discharge summary has been reviewed with the patient and son Quang via Vietnamese interpreter; they confirm understanding.
attestation:
Electronically signed by:
Dr. Anna Reyes, MD (PGY-2, Internal Medicine) — primary author
Dr. Jennifer Beckman, MD (Attending Hospitalist) — reviewing attending
Date: 4/12/2027, 8:00 AM
